meta.reviewer: Claude (Opus 4.8 / Fable 5) via Claude Code weekly review
meta.coverage: Core tier, top 15 by priority score
meta.disclaimer: Second-opinion research notes on @aleabitoreddit's ideas. Personal research tool — not investment advice.

verdicts.view: The washout resolved his way on three fronts at once: a CEO 1M-SEK open-market buy (plus two more insider purchases) right after the drop, Nvidia denying the delay reports that caused it, and a sell-side channel check saying Chinese rivals are 'quite far' from high-power CW lasers — the exact scarcity his thesis rests on. Insider buying after a misleading-headline drop is the strongest single-name signal this desk has seen on SIVE; the residual risk is unchanged (pre-volume-ramp execution, and he is heavily talking his book).
verdicts.execution: Treat this as the add-on-dip tranche the staggered plan reserved: the insider cluster plus Rosenblatt's note that shorts intend to cover in late July/August suggests the discount window is closing. Size it as one planned tranche, not a rewrite of the plan.
verdicts.changesMind: Insider selling resuming, the NASDAQ listing slipping without explanation, or the GFS/JBL partnerships failing to convert into volume orders by the stated ramp window.

verdicts.view: The channel check is the week's substance: Chinese laser makers reportedly have no working 200G EMLs and are far from high-power CWs — which keeps LITE's sold-out-into-2029 position intact rather than contested. Rosenblatt adds that the short sellers pressuring the sector don't believe their own thesis and plan to cover in late July/August. Agreeing with him here is easy; the harder question stays valuation on the eventual re-rate.
verdicts.execution: Keep building the lower-beta anchor on red days — the short-cover timeline argues the red days are numbered. No sizing change.
verdicts.changesMind: Hyperscaler order pushouts substantiating the CPO-delay bear case, or share loss to the CW-laser entrants it currently buys from.

verdicts.view: Unchanged as the gravitational center rather than a pick. This week it appears as collateral damage in the narrative war (SemiAnalysis supply-chain bear posts helped trigger the selloff) and in the Hesai lidar scrutiny story. His edge — and this tool's — remains the layers beneath it.
verdicts.execution: Express the view through the suppliers this map surfaces; hold NVDA as a separate core-portfolio decision outside this workflow.
verdicts.changesMind: N/A — re-rate if he starts publishing NVDA-specific supply/pricing work.

verdicts.view: The bull/bear fight is now explicit and useful: B. Riley's sell case says network flattening cuts transceiver TAM 40-50%; Rosenblatt's counter is that XPU count grows faster than per-XPU optics shrink, so total demand still rises. His $471m/month H2-2027 revenue framing survives the week unchallenged by anything new. The desk keeps siding with the volume argument while flagging that AAOI is now a named battleground stock — expect headline whipsaw into earnings.
verdicts.execution: Hold the scale-in from the washout; keep the reserved half for the earnings print. The sell-report noise is not a thesis change, but it does raise the odds the print is the decisive event.
verdicts.changesMind: An earnings miss against the monthly run-rate narrative, or the AMD CW LTA relationship failing to expand — either sends this back to watch.

verdicts.view: The most concrete new datapoint of the week attaches here: per his relay of a JPM fireside chat, JBL's 1.6T LRO has a 'relatively dramatic moat' with qualification completing roughly Aug 2026–Mar 2027, then mass production using Sivers lasers in pluggables. That turns the conservative-expression thesis into one with a dated catalyst window.
verdicts.execution: Starter position stands; the qualification window gives you a concrete thing to verify in the next two earnings calls — add if optics contribution starts getting broken out or the qual completes on schedule.
verdicts.changesMind: Qualification slipping past Q1 2027, losing transceiver program share, or optics staying too small to move a $28B revenue base.

verdicts.view: Slightly firmer calendar (production starting H2 2026, more in H1 2027) but the same unnamed-customer problem. The sharpest new datapoint is relative: he flags that SIVE — which supplies lasers to POET among others — trades below POET, which is as much a POET-richness observation as a SIVE-cheapness one. The disciplined trigger is unchanged.
verdicts.execution: Unchanged: speculative sizing only. Trigger remains a named hyperscaler customer or actual HVM revenue in the H2 2026 prints. If you want the exposure, his own relative-value logic argues the supplier is the cheaper way in.
verdicts.changesMind: A confirmed, named hyperscaler customer (upgrades it), or H2 2026 passing without the promised HVM ramp (kills it).

verdicts.view: Still no standalone thesis — this week's only appearance is in the indiscriminate-selloff list. The NVDA-backed-ASIC-challenger frame from last week remains speculative and uncorroborated. The tiering math promotes it; the posts don't.
verdicts.execution: No action on this analyst's signal alone. Revisit on a dedicated MRVL bottleneck post or hard evidence of the NVDA-alliance theory.
verdicts.changesMind: A dedicated post with a specific Marvell design-win or bottleneck claim, or independent confirmation of the NVDA-backed ASIC-challenger pattern.

verdicts.view: Same shape as last week: the platform beneath the photonics thesis (SCALE reference laser, CPO timelines now sketched 'somewhere in 2027'), still mentioned only inside SIVE-centric posts, still no standalone valuation work. Deliberately at watch despite Core tier.
verdicts.execution: No dedicated action — SIVE/JBL positions already carry the GFS-platform exposure. Revisit on disclosed photonics revenue or a named hyperscaler CPO win.
verdicts.changesMind: A dedicated GFS thesis with numbers, or disclosed silicon-photonics revenue making the platform story investable on its own.

verdicts.view: No new neocloud-specific thesis this week — NBIS simply led his indiscriminate-selloff list, which he reads as margin cascades. But the fact-check reframes the catalyst he waved off: the 1 Jul trigger was not 'Meta cutting capex' (Meta had raised 2026 guidance to $125-145B); it was Bloomberg reporting Meta will sell its excess compute — i.e. Meta becoming a neocloud competitor, which hit CoreWeave -14% and NBIS -17% specifically. That is a real structural threat to neocloud pricing power, not FUD. The financing-heavy, contract-concentrated caveat therefore matters more, not less.
verdicts.execution: Hold, but treat this as the most fundamentally-exposed name in the selloff, not a clean technical dip. Add only on disclosed hyperscaler contract expansions that reprice committed capacity (the TeraWulf/Anthropic comp), not on red days alone — and watch whether Meta's compute-selling ambitions actually land.
verdicts.changesMind: Hyperscaler capex guidance cuts, Meta (or another hyperscaler) materially undercutting neocloud pricing with resold compute, META insourcing rented capacity, or a debt raise that changes the risk math.

verdicts.view: His capex narrative won the week outright: a published internal memo shows LTAs with Samsung and SNDK for memory and Sumitomo for fiber, 7GW deployed this year doubling in 2027, up to $145B capex, and a new chip every six months — followed by a new $10B Canadian datacenter. The 'Meta is cutting' framing that triggered the whole selloff is now directly contradicted by Meta's own documents. Fair credit: he called this while it was contrarian.
verdicts.execution: The position here is really the supply chain it validates — photonics, memory, neoclouds. Keep META itself as the accumulate-on-weakness hyperscaler leg; the memo removes the main bear scenario.
verdicts.changesMind: A guided capex cut, the Zuckerberg 'agents haven't accelerated' concession hardening into slower buildout, or 'Watermelon' underdelivering enough to revive the retreat narrative.

verdicts.view: Three new mentions but all inside list-posts plus a bare 'still holding' — the mention count flatters the signal (focus-weighting exists for exactly this). The substance is unchanged: his '40% of the InP substrate supply chain' framing, feeding the same photonics ramp. Last week's downgrade-on-price stands; nothing this week re-rates it.
verdicts.execution: Stay on watch. The InP chokepoint story is real but already moved; a fresh entry wants either a pullback toward the pre-run range or a new dedicated thesis with numbers, not list-post momentum.
verdicts.changesMind: China export licenses and AXT's capacity additions normalizing InP ASPs, or another dilutive raise into strength (kills the case); a deep retrace (30-40% toward the run's base) with the Coherent-style committed-capacity deals still expanding (re-opens accumulation).

verdicts.view: His conviction escalated to 'trying to own as much % of Agility as possible' — maximum talking-his-book territory, so discount accordingly. The substantive adds: a RoboFab at 10K+ units/year with 75% US-sourced components (versus TSLA's China-dependent BOM), and the China-ships-90%-of-humanoids stat that makes a US champion politically valuable. Real theme, real asset — but a SPAC structure at ~$3.9B premoney in a euphoria phase is exactly where anchoring errors get expensive.
verdicts.execution: Stay watch. If it must be owned, size it as venture risk (small, no adds on spikes) and wait for the post-SPAC float to settle; the Amazon/MELI commercialization milestones are the real triggers.
verdicts.changesMind: A funded, dated Amazon deployment contract or US robotics subsidy program (upgrades it); dilution at the AGLT ticker change, the SPAC closing below the cited valuation, or China price-war spillover crushing unit economics (downgrades it).

verdicts.view: The physical-AI-umbrella thesis gained a concrete edge: he frames Amazon (with MELI) as the deployment supply chain through which Agility commercializes — the demand-side anchor for his robotics book, replaying the GOOGL-TPU-catalyzes-suppliers pattern. Lowest-risk expression of a theme every smaller name here depends on.
verdicts.execution: Core-position logic, dollar-cost average — unchanged. Still the hedge against the CCXI-style venture bets: if the robotics rotation cools, this falls least.
verdicts.changesMind: The robotics/automation effort visibly stalling (deployment numbers, not narrative), or AWS capex guidance breaking the AI-demand story.

verdicts.view: New to Core on the robotics rotation. His take is directional, not fundamental: Elon is the reason the US leads in space and EVs, so he 'can make US robotics #1 again' — and he notes TSLA's $1T+ valuation is itself anchored to the humanoid mission. Revealingly, he owns CCXI instead, calling Agility the purer mass-production play. When the analyst's own money picks the smaller name, TSLA is his theme index, not his idea.
verdicts.execution: No action from this signal — TSLA here is a robotics-sentiment gauge, and any Tesla position is a whole-company bet (autos, energy, FSD) far beyond this corpus. If you want his actual robotics idea, that's the CCXI verdict, venture-sized.
verdicts.changesMind: A dedicated Optimus production/economics thesis from him, or TSLA disclosures making humanoid economics separable from the auto business.

verdicts.view: New to the verdict roster. Two theses stack here: a value floor — Kepler's upgrade calls it the cheapest specialty foundry at 4.8x 2027 EV/EBITDA versus a 14.5x peer median, with Melexis demand strengthening weekly — and a free option, the photonixfab silicon-photonics line he thinks the market can't price until volume contracts land (NVDA/Nokia evaluations, EU Chips Act backing). He's called it 'early TSEM' for months; the sell side just started agreeing on the boring half.
verdicts.execution: The value leg justifies a real (not speculative) starter position; the photonics leg is the free upside. European listing and specialty-foundry cyclicality argue for patience over chasing — accumulate on flat-to-red days.
verdicts.changesMind: Auto/industrial demand rolling back over (the Kepler leg breaking), or photonixfab losing the NVDA/Nokia evaluations.
